issues.original: I keep getting asked "is OpenClaw just another wrapper around an LLM?" The short answer is no, and the reason matters enough that I wanted to write it down before walking through the QuickStart.
issues.fixed: I keep getting asked, "Is OpenClaw just another wrapper around an LLM?" The short answer is no, and the reason is important enough that I wanted to write it down before walking through the QuickStart.

issues.original: This is the first piece in a six-part series. By the end you should have a working OpenClaw on your own machine, talking to a model, listening on at least one chat channel, and doing something useful that survives a reboot.
issues.fixed: This is the first part of a six-part series. By the end, you should have a working OpenClaw on your machine, communicating with a model, listening on at least one chat channel, and performing tasks that survive a reboot.

issues.original: Before diving in, here is what I expect you to already have:
issues.fixed: Before diving in, here's what I expect you to already have:

issues.original: You should be comfortable with a terminal, editing config files, and reading TypeScript or JavaScript stack traces. You don't need to write code for this series, but when something breaks you will read an error and know whether it is a network timeout or a syntax issue.
issues.fixed: You should be comfortable with a terminal, editing config files, and reading TypeScript or JavaScript stack traces. You don't need to write code for this series, but when something breaks, you should be able to read an error and determine if it's a network timeout or a syntax issue.

issues.original: OpenClaw runs on Node 18 or later. If you don't have Node installed yet, or you are on an older version, the next article will walk through installation. For now, just know that [CODE] should show at least [CODE].
issues.fixed: OpenClaw runs on Node 18 or later. If you don't have Node installed or are using an older version, the next article will guide you through the installation. For now, just know that [CODE] should show at least [CODE].

issues.original: You will need at least one working API key for a language model provider. The series shows setup for three options — Alibaba Cloud's Bailian Coding Plan (free for students and verified developers), Claude direct (paid, requires Anthropic API access), and DashScope free tier (limited quota but requires no credit card). Pick whichever fits your situation. If you have none of these yet, I will show you where to sign up in article two.
issues.fixed: You will need at least one working API key from a language model provider. The series covers three options: Alibaba Cloud's Bailian Coding Plan (free for students and verified developers), Claude Direct (paid, requires Anthropic API access), and DashScope Free Tier (limited quota but no credit card required). Choose the one that fits your situation. If you don't have any of these yet, I'll show you where to sign up in article two.

issues.original: This series uses Telegram because the setup is trivial — create a bot via BotFather, copy the token, done. You don't need a Telegram account yet, but you will want one by article four. If you prefer DingTalk, WeChat, or Discord, the concepts transfer directly; I just won't hold your hand through their registration flows.
issues.fixed: This series uses Telegram because the setup is simple: create a bot via BotFather, copy the token, and you're done. You don't need a Telegram account yet, but you will want one by article four. If you prefer DingTalk, WeChat, or Discord, the concepts transfer directly; I just won't guide you through their registration processes.

issues.original: If the idea of SSH-ing into a VPS or editing a [CODE] file makes you uncomfortable, you might be happier with a hosted solution like ChatGPT Plus or Claude Pro. No judgment — those are excellent products. But if you are here because you want the agent on your infrastructure, reading your local files, and not phoning home, then you are in the right place.
issues.fixed: If SSH-ing into a VPS or editing a [CODE] file makes you uncomfortable, you might prefer a hosted solution like ChatGPT Plus or Claude Pro. No judgment—those are excellent products. But if you want the agent on your infrastructure, reading your local files, and not phoning home, you're in the right place.

issues.original: OpenClaw is a self-hosted AI agent platform. You install a single Node binary, give it API keys for one or more LLM providers, and it runs a long-lived gateway process that listens for messages on whatever chat platforms you wire up — DingTalk, WeChat (via WorkBuddy), Telegram, Discord, Slack, Feishu, you pick. The gateway routes those messages through an agent loop with tools, skills, memory, and a cron scheduler. The whole thing is MIT-licensed, the data lives on your machine, and you can swap models without rewriting your prompts.
issues.fixed: OpenClaw is a self-hosted AI agent platform. You install a single Node binary, provide API keys for one or more LLM providers, and it runs a long-lived gateway process that listens for messages on various chat platforms—DingTalk, WeChat (via WorkBuddy), Telegram, Discord, Slack, Feishu, and more. The gateway routes these messages through an agent loop with tools, skills, memory, and a cron scheduler. The entire system is MIT-licensed, the data stays on your machine, and you can switch models without rewriting your prompts.

issues.original: If you have used Claude Code or Cursor, the agent loop will feel familiar. The difference is that those products own the surface — you talk to them in their UI. OpenClaw inverts that. The surface is whatever messenger you already use. The agent comes to you.
issues.fixed: If you've used Claude Code or Cursor, the agent loop will feel familiar. The difference is that those products control the interface—you interact with them through their UI. OpenClaw inverts this. The interface is whatever messenger you already use. The agent comes to you.

issues.original: When a message arrives from any channel, the gateway deserializes it into a normalized conversation turn. That turn gets passed to the agent core, which decides whether to invoke a skill, call a tool, query memory, or just generate a reply. The agent core is model-agnostic — it speaks to LLM providers through a unified interface, so swapping from Claude to Qwen to GPT-4 is a config change, not a refactor. The response flows back through the channel adapter, which serializes it into whatever format that platform expects — Markdown for Telegram, interactive cards for DingTalk, plain text for SMS.
issues.fixed: When a message arrives from any channel, the gateway deserializes it into a normalized conversation turn. This turn is passed to the agent core, which decides whether to invoke a skill, call a tool, query memory, or generate a reply. The agent core is model-agnostic—it communicates with LLM providers through a unified interface, so switching from Claude to Qwen to GPT-4 is a configuration change, not a refactor. The response flows back through the channel adapter, which serializes it into the format expected by the platform—Markdown for Telegram, interactive cards for DingTalk, and plain text for SMS.

issues.original: Inside the core, every turn goes through a four-stage cycle: Memory (load context for this user and conversation), Planning (decide what to do next based on the task and available tools), Tool execution (run filesystem ops, web searches, API calls, or MCP servers), and Reflection (evaluate whether the task is done or needs another iteration). This is not a novel architecture — it is the same loop that powers Cursor's agent mode and Claude Code's autonomous workflow. The difference is that OpenClaw exposes every stage as a hook you can customize or observe.
issues.fixed: Inside the core, every turn goes through a four-stage cycle: Memory (load context for the user and conversation), Planning (decide the next action based on the task and available tools), Tool Execution (run filesystem operations, web searches, API calls, or MCP servers), and Reflection (evaluate whether the task is complete or needs another iteration). This architecture is not novel—it's the same loop that powers Cursor's agent mode and Claude Code's autonomous workflow. The difference is that OpenClaw exposes each stage as a hook you can customize or observe.

issues.original: Each chat platform gets its own adapter module. The adapter knows how to authenticate, maintain a connection, parse incoming events, and serialize outgoing messages. Some platforms use webhooks (Slack, Discord), some use long-polling (Telegram), some use persistent WebSocket streams (DingTalk). The adapter abstracts all of this. From the agent core's perspective, it is just sending and receiving structured JSON blobs. This separation is why adding a new channel usually takes a day or two instead of a week — you implement one interface and the rest just works.
issues.fixed: Each chat platform has its own adapter module. The adapter handles authentication, maintains connections, parses incoming events, and serializes outgoing messages. Some platforms use webhooks (Slack, Discord), some use long-polling (Telegram), and some use persistent WebSocket streams (DingTalk). The adapter abstracts all of this. From the agent core's perspective, it's just sending and receiving structured JSON blobs. This separation is why adding a new channel usually takes a day or two instead of a week—you implement one interface, and the rest just works.

issues.original: Skills are the agent's vocabulary. A skill is a Markdown file that describes a task, optional helper scripts, and a manifest that declares when the skill should be offered to the agent. At runtime, only the manifest is loaded into memory. The full skill body is paged in lazily when the agent matches the task to the skill. This keeps the system prompt small and lets you accumulate dozens of skills without blowing your context window. I will show you how to write a custom skill in article five.
issues.fixed: Skills are the agent's vocabulary. A skill is a Markdown file that describes a task, optional helper scripts, and a manifest that specifies when the skill should be offered to the agent. At runtime, only the manifest is loaded into memory. The full skill body is loaded lazily when the agent matches the task to the skill. This keeps the system prompt small and allows you to accumulate dozens of skills without exceeding the context window. I'll show you how to write a custom skill in article five.

issues.original: DashScope is the path of least resistance if you are in mainland China. Latency to their endpoints from any domestic network is under 200ms. No proxy, no VPN, no fuss.
issues.fixed: DashScope is the easiest option if you're in mainland China. Latency to their endpoints from any domestic network is under 200ms. No proxy, no VPN, no fuss.

issues.original: If you are in mainland China: DashScope endpoints are domestic and fast. Anthropic and OpenAI endpoints require either a proxy, a Hong Kong VPS forwarding requests, or a SOCKS5 tunnel. OpenClaw respects [CODE] and [CODE] environment variables, so if you already have a proxy running locally you can export those before starting the gateway. Do not waste time debugging 'connection timeout' errors before checking whether you can actually reach the provider's endpoint from your network.
issues.fixed: If you're in mainland China, DashScope endpoints are domestic and fast. Anthropic and OpenAI endpoints require a proxy, a Hong Kong VPS, or a SOCKS5 tunnel. OpenClaw respects [CODE] and [CODE] environment variables, so if you have a local proxy, export them before starting the gateway. Don't waste time debugging 'connection timeout' errors without checking if you can reach the provider's endpoint from your network.

issues.original: You get a chat-like terminal UI. Try a few things in order: Three things should happen: 1. The first message returns a one-liner — model is talking. 2. The second triggers the [CODE] tool — the agent asks the gateway to read a file, and you see a tool-call line scroll past in the gateway log. 3. The third actually mutates your filesystem. Verify with [CODE].
issues.fixed: You get a chat-like terminal UI. Try a few things in order: 1. The first message returns a one-liner — the model is talking. 2. The second triggers the [CODE] tool — the agent asks the gateway to read a file, and you see a tool-call line in the gateway log. 3. The third modifies your filesystem. Verify with [CODE].

issues.original: If all three worked, the install is done. If only the first worked, the agent isn't getting access to tools — most likely the model you picked is too small to do tool-calling reliably. Switch to [CODE] or [CODE] and try again.
issues.fixed: If all three worked, the install is complete. If only the first worked, the agent likely lacks access to tools, probably because the model is too small for reliable tool-calling. Switch to [CODE] or [CODE] and try again.

issues.original: This uses the [CODE] tool. The gateway log shows: The agent sees the stdout and reasons over it. If the exit code is non-zero, it will tell you what went wrong.
issues.fixed: This uses the [CODE] tool. The gateway log shows the agent's reasoning over the stdout. If the exit code is non-zero, it will tell you what went wrong.

issues.original: For a multi-step task: Watch the gateway log — you will see multiple tool calls chained: an [CODE] to grep, then several [CODE] calls to inspect individual files, then the final synthesis. This is the agent loop doing what it does: plan, act, observe, repeat.
issues.fixed: For a multi-step task, watch the gateway log. You'll see multiple tool calls chained: an [CODE] to grep, followed by several [CODE] calls to inspect individual files, and finally the synthesis. This is the agent loop: plan, act, observe, repeat.

issues.original: You can use OpenClaw for months without reading this piece. But the first time you try to write a skill, debug a misrouted message, or figure out why the agent forgot something, you will want to know what each box does.
issues.fixed: You can use OpenClaw for months without reading this. But the first time you need to write a skill, debug a misrouted message, or figure out why the agent forgot something, you'll want to know what each component does.

issues.original: A Channel is the code that turns 'a DingTalk Stream message' into 'a normalized OpenClaw message' and vice versa. Each channel has its own quirks: DingTalk sends Stream events over a WebSocket, Telegram polls or webhooks, Discord uses a Gateway WebSocket of its own. The Channel layer hides all of that.
issues.fixed: A Channel converts 'a DingTalk Stream message' into 'a normalized OpenClaw message' and vice versa. Each channel has its quirks: DingTalk sends Stream events over a WebSocket, Telegram uses polling or webhooks, and Discord uses a Gateway WebSocket. The Channel layer abstracts these differences.

issues.original: What you need to remember: - Channels are configured per-instance. You can run with zero, one, or twenty. - A message goes Channel → Gateway → Agent → Gateway → Channel. The Channel doesn't talk to the Agent directly. - Per-channel rate limits and quirks live in the channel adapter — that's why DingTalk replies feel different from Telegram replies.
issues.fixed: Remember: - Channels are configured per instance. You can run with zero, one, or twenty. - A message flows through Channel → Gateway → Agent → Gateway → Channel. The Channel doesn't communicate directly with the Agent. - Per-channel rate limits and quirks are handled by the channel adapter, which is why DingTalk and Telegram replies differ.

issues.original: The Gateway runs on [CODE]. It accepts messages from any channel, deduplicates them (DingTalk sometimes redelivers), assigns or restores a session, and hands the message to the Router. The Gateway is also the only thing that talks to the model provider. Every tool result, every memory read, every prompt assembly goes through it. That's why you only need one set of API keys.
issues.fixed: The Gateway runs on [CODE]. It accepts messages from any channel, deduplicates them (DingTalk sometimes redelivers), assigns or restores a session, and passes the message to the Router. The Gateway is the only component that communicates with the model provider. All tool results, memory reads, and prompt assemblies go through it, so you only need one set of API keys.

issues.original: If ten users send messages simultaneously, the Gateway serializes LLM calls but doesn't throttle ingress. You will see HTTP 429 from the provider, which the Gateway retries with exponential backoff (max 3 retries). If all three fail, the user gets 'I'm having trouble thinking right now' and the turn is logged to [CODE]. The fix is either upgrading your provider tier or configuring [CODE] in [CODE] to match your quota.
issues.fixed: If ten users send messages simultaneously, the Gateway serializes LLM calls but doesn't throttle incoming requests. You'll see HTTP 429 from the provider, which the Gateway retries with exponential backoff (up to 3 retries). If all retries fail, the user gets 'I'm having trouble thinking right now,' and the turn is logged to [CODE]. To fix this, upgrade your provider tier or configure [CODE] in [CODE] to match your quota.

issues.original: The Router decides *which* agent should handle a message — relevant only if you've configured multiple agents (the default install has one, called Pi). Sessions are how OpenClaw keeps a conversation in WeChat distinct from a conversation in Telegram even though they go to the same agent. Session ID is [CODE].
issues.fixed: The Router determines which agent should handle a message — relevant only if you have multiple agents (the default install has one, called Pi). Sessions help OpenClaw keep conversations in WeChat separate from those in Telegram, even if they go to the same agent. The Session ID is [CODE].

issues.original: If you have ever seen 'the agent confused two of my conversations', that is a session-ID collision and almost always a custom-channel bug.
issues.fixed: If you've ever seen 'the agent confused two of my conversations,' that's a session-ID collision, usually caused by a custom-channel bug.

issues.original: The interesting choices OpenClaw makes here: - **Skills are loaded lazily.** Only the manifest is in the system prompt. The body is paged in when the model triggers a skill. This keeps token cost low. - **Tool errors are returned to the model, not raised.** The model gets a chance to recover. This sounds obvious but a lot of agent frameworks just throw. - **The loop has a hard turn-limit.** Default 30. If the agent is still looping at turn 30 it stops and emits a 'I think I'm stuck' message rather than burning your token budget overnight.
issues.fixed: OpenClaw makes these key choices: - **Skills are loaded lazily.** Only the manifest is in the system prompt. The body is paged in when the model triggers a skill, keeping token costs low. - **Tool errors are returned to the model, not raised.** This gives the model a chance to recover. Many agent frameworks simply throw errors. - **The loop has a hard turn limit.** The default is 30. If the agent is still looping at turn 30, it stops and emits an 'I think I'm stuck' message to avoid burning your token budget overnight.

issues.original: If you only edit one file in OpenClaw, this is it. [CODE] controls models, tools, channels, memory, cron, and skill loading. The onboarding wizard wrote a default; this piece walks the parts you will actually touch.
issues.fixed: If you only edit one file in OpenClaw, this is it. [CODE] controls models, tools, channels, memory, cron, and skill loading. The onboarding wizard created a default; this guide covers the parts you'll actually use.

issues.original: The mistake people make is putting the most expensive model here, 'to be safe.' Don't. The planning loop runs on every turn. Put [CODE] here, and override to [CODE] only inside specific skills that need real reasoning.
issues.fixed: The common mistake is using the most expensive model here, 'to be safe.' Don't. The planning loop runs on every turn. Use [CODE] here and override to [CODE] only for specific skills that need real reasoning.

issues.fixed: OpenAI models are useful in two cases: (1) you need GPT-5.4 specifically, or (2) your skill was prototyped with OpenAI and you don't want to rewrite. Otherwise, I avoid them — Claude is better for reasoning, and Qwen is cheaper for bulk work.

issues.original: The relationship: Skills *use* tools. MCP servers *provide* tools. A skill might say 'use the Playwright tools to scrape this page' — the Playwright tools come from an MCP server, and the skill tells the agent what to do with them.
issues.fixed: The relationship: Skills use tools. MCP servers provide tools. A skill might say, 'use the Playwright tools to scrape this page.' The Playwright tools come from an MCP server, and the skill tells the agent what to do with them.

issues.original: Think of it as: Tools are the hands. Skills are the training manual. MCP is a way to give the agent more hands.
issues.fixed: Think of it like this: Tools are the hands. Skills are the training manual. MCP is a way to give the agent more hands.

issues.original: MCP — Model Context Protocol — is a standard for connecting LLMs to external tool servers. OpenClaw doesn't speak MCP natively; it uses [CODE] as a shim that translates between OpenClaw's internal tool format and the MCP protocol.
issues.fixed: MCP, or Model Context Protocol, is a standard for connecting LLMs to external tool servers. OpenClaw doesn't use MCP natively; it uses [CODE] as a shim to translate between OpenClaw's internal tool format and the MCP protocol.

issues.original: Two files matter the most. [CODE] is the index — the agent reads it on every turn, so it has to be short. [CODE] is where the long tail of 'we tried that, it broke because Y' lives. The daily logs are write-heavy and read-rarely; the agent only reaches for them through search.

issues.original: OpenClaw recognizes five memory types internally. You don't have to use all of them, but understanding the taxonomy helps you route new information correctly.

issues.original: The decision flowchart I use: Did the user correct you? That's feedback, goes to [CODE] with a date. Is it a fact about the user as a person? User memory, goes in [CODE]. About the current state of a task? Project memory, goes in [CODE]. A stable reference like an IP or endpoint? Reference, goes in [CODE]. A generalizable insight from failure? Lesson, goes in [CODE]. None of the above? Probably noise — daily log or discard.
issues.fixed: The decision flowchart I use: Did the user correct you? That's feedback, goes to [CODE] with a date. Is it a fact about the user as a person? User memory, goes in [CODE]. About the current state of a task? Project memory, goes in [CODE]. A stable reference like an IP or endpoint? Reference, goes in [CODE]. A generalizable insight from failure? Lesson, goes in [CODE]. None of the above? Probably noise — daily log or discard.

issues.original: When in doubt, write it to the daily log. If you find yourself searching for it later, promote it to the right tier.
issues.fixed: When in doubt, write it to the daily log. If you find yourself searching for it later, promote it to the right tier.

issues.original: Long conversations get compacted automatically when the context window gets tight. By default, the agent loses whatever it didn't write down. [CODE] runs a 'save the important stuff' pass *before* compaction:
issues.fixed: Long conversations get compacted automatically when the context window gets tight. By default, the agent loses whatever it didn't write down. [CODE] runs a 'save the important stuff' pass before compaction:

issues.original: [CODE] is how much room the flush itself can take. 4000 is enough for a useful summary; 8000 is wasteful unless you're doing very long sessions.
issues.fixed: [CODE] determines how much room the flush can take. 4000 is enough for a useful summary; 8000 is wasteful unless you're doing very long sessions.

issues.original: Here's a real scenario from before I turned this on. I was 80 turns into a debugging session. Around turn 20, I mentioned switching my embedding provider from OpenAI to SiliconFlow due to rate limits. The agent acknowledged it, we moved on.
issues.fixed: Here's a real scenario from before I turned this on. I was 80 turns into a debugging session. Around turn 20, I mentioned switching my embedding provider from OpenAI to SiliconFlow due to rate limits. The agent acknowledged it, and we moved on.

issues.original: Around turn 60, context got tight. Compaction trimmed the middle (turns 15-45). The provider switch lived there. Gone. Turn 70, I asked the agent to re-index. It called OpenAI. Failed. Ten minutes burned on something that should have been permanent knowledge.
issues.fixed: Around turn 60, context got tight. Compaction trimmed the middle (turns 15-45), removing the provider switch. At turn 70, I asked the agent to re-index. It called OpenAI and failed. Ten minutes wasted on something that should have been permanent knowledge.

issues.original: With [CODE] enabled, the flush pass fires first — writing 'Embedding provider changed to SiliconFlow, OpenAI deprecated' into [CODE] before trimming. Post-compaction, the agent reloads and picks it up.
issues.fixed: With [CODE] enabled, the flush pass writes 'Embedding provider changed to SiliconFlow, OpenAI deprecated' into [CODE] before trimming. Post-compaction, the agent reloads and picks it up.

issues.original:
- **Without memoryFlush:** compaction deletes silently. Agent reverts to stale assumptions.
- **With memoryFlush:** compaction triggers a write pass first. Critical facts persist.
issues.fixed:
- **Without memoryFlush:** compaction deletes silently. The agent reverts to stale assumptions.
- **With memoryFlush:** compaction triggers a write pass first. Critical facts persist.

issues.original: You can run semantic search across the memory files. You need an embedding API. The cheap path is SiliconFlow's [CODE], which is free and good enough:
issues.fixed: You can run semantic search across the memory files. You need an embedding API. The cheap option is SiliconFlow's [CODE], which is free and good enough:

issues.original: You'll only see the win after about a week of usage. Before that there's nothing to search.
issues.fixed: You'll only see the benefit after about a week of usage. Before that, there's nothing to search.

issues.original: Every token spent on memory is a token not spent on conversation. Here's the math.
issues.fixed: Every token spent on memory is a token not spent on conversation. Here's the math.

issues.original: [CODE] at 40 lines costs about 600 tokens — your fixed overhead per turn. Each file chunk from [CODE] costs 200-500 tokens. Default retrieval budget is 2000 tokens, meaning 3-4 chunks per turn.
issues.fixed: [CODE] at 40 lines costs about 600 tokens — your fixed overhead per turn. Each file chunk from [CODE] costs 200-500 tokens. The default retrieval budget is 2000 tokens, meaning 3-4 chunks per turn.

issues.original: Total overhead per turn: 600 + 2000 = 2600 tokens. On a 128k model, about 2%. Acceptable.
issues.fixed: Total overhead per turn: 600 + 2000 = 2600 tokens. On a 128k model, that's about 2%. Acceptable.

issues.original: Tune with [CODE]:
issues.fixed: Tune with [CODE]:

issues.original: Why not crank it to 4000 or higher? Because memory competes with the conversation. At 4000, you're spending 4600 tokens on overhead. In a multi-turn conversation with a 6000-token reply budget, nearly half is eaten by retrieval. The agent's responses get squeezed.
issues.fixed: Why not set it to 4000 or higher? Memory competes with the conversation. At 4000, you're spending 4600 tokens on overhead. In a multi-turn conversation with a 6000-token reply budget, nearly half is used for retrieval. The agent's responses get squeezed.

issues.original: The sweet spot: 1000 for quick tasks (< 10 turns), 2000 for standard sessions (the default), 3000 for long research. Never above 4000 — it crowds out conversation. If the agent misses context at 2000, the fix is better memory entries, not a bigger budget.
issues.fixed: The sweet spot: 1000 for quick tasks (< 10 turns), 2000 for standard sessions (the default), and 3000 for long research. Never go above 4000 — it crowds out conversation. If the agent misses context at 2000, improve the memory entries, not the budget.

issues.original: Up to v2026.3.6, memory was an explicit tool the agent had to call. If it forgot, it operated without context. From v2026.3.7, memory moved into a hooks lifecycle the harness runs around the agent:
issues.fixed: Up to v2026.3.6, memory was an explicit tool the agent had to call. If it forgot, it operated without context. From v2026.3.7, memory moved into a hooks lifecycle the harness runs around the agent:

issues.original: This chapter is the sequel for everyone in mainland China who needed to ship something past their team's IT department. There are too many channels, the docs are scattered across a dozen READMEs, and most of the 'compare' tables online are out of date.
issues.fixed: This chapter is the sequel for everyone in mainland China who needs to get something past their team's IT department. There are too many channels, the docs are scattered across a dozen READMEs, and most of the 'compare' tables online are outdated.

issues.original: What follows is the matrix I check before recommending anything to anyone.
issues.fixed: Here’s the matrix I check before making any recommendations.

issues.original: Then the choices:
issues.fixed: Here are the choices:

issues.original: Two notes: Feishu is officially listed as deprecated for new openclaw-china installs as of March 2026 — pick DingTalk or WeCom. WeChat public accounts in subscription mode have a 5-second passive reply window and cannot push proactively; service accounts and test accounts don't.
issues.fixed: Two notes: Feishu is officially deprecated for new openclaw-china installs as of March 2026 — choose DingTalk or WeCom. WeChat public accounts in subscription mode have a 5-second passive reply window and can't push proactively; service accounts and test accounts can.

issues.original: Latency numbers are round-trip message-to-response timings measured from Beijing/Shanghai during business hours. The biggest latency killer is the webhook delivery path — public accounts and WeCom self-built apps route through Tencent's dispatcher, adding 500-1000ms compared to long-poll channels.
issues.fixed: Latency numbers are round-trip message-to-response timings measured from Beijing/Shanghai during business hours. The main cause of high latency is the webhook delivery path — public accounts and WeCom self-built apps route through Tencent's dispatcher, adding 500-1000ms compared to long-poll channels.

issues.original: Message format support matters more than it looks. Markdown means your agent can send code blocks and formatted lists. DingTalk and WeCom smart bot handle GitHub-flavored markdown well. WeChat channels require plain text or proprietary card schemas.
issues.fixed: Message format support is more important than it seems. Markdown allows your agent to send code blocks and formatted lists. DingTalk and WeCom smart bot handle GitHub-flavored markdown well. WeChat channels require plain text or proprietary card schemas.

issues.original: Don't read the matrix top to bottom. Ask yourself three questions:
issues.fixed: Instead of reading the matrix top to bottom, ask yourself three questions:

issues.original: In practice I land on one of two stacks:
issues.fixed: In practice, I usually end up with one of two stacks:

issues.original: Stream mode (long-poll) is the only reason DingTalk is fast to ship. No public IP, no domain verification, just register a bot and grab two values:
issues.fixed: Stream mode (long-poll) is why DingTalk is quick to set up. No public IP or domain verification required—just register a bot and grab two values:

issues.original: The most common failure: messages stop arriving after 30 minutes. Cause: the org's network egress is rotating IPs and the long-poll connection is being killed mid-stream. Fix: bump the reconnect interval down to 60s in [CODE], and put the Gateway behind a stable egress.
issues.fixed: The most common issue: messages stop arriving after 30 minutes. This happens because the organization's network egress rotates IPs, killing the long-poll connection mid-stream. Fix: reduce the reconnect interval to 60s in [CODE] and place the Gateway behind a stable egress.

issues.original: For 90% of 'we need a bot in our team' cases, the smart bot is what you want.
issues.fixed: For 90% of 'we need a bot in our team' cases, the smart bot is the best choice.

issues.original: The local install gets you to "it works on my machine." The server install is what makes it survive a kernel update.
issues.fixed: The local install gets you to 'it works on my machine.' The server install ensures it survives a kernel update.

issues.original: This chapter walks through the deploy I actually use on a 2-core 4G ECS box, then the failures I have seen often enough to put in writing.
issues.fixed: This chapter walks through the deployment I use on a 2-core 4GB ECS box and the common failures I've documented.

issues.original: Before you deploy, pick the right box. Four options worth considering:
issues.fixed: Before deploying, choose the right server. Consider these four options:

issues.original: The advantage is proximity to DashScope — your API round-trips drop from 200ms to 20ms when both the gateway and the model sit in the same region. The disadvantage is the Great Firewall makes outbound package installs occasionally flaky unless you set up a mirror.
issues.fixed: The advantage is proximity to DashScope, reducing API round-trips from 200ms to 20ms when both the gateway and the model are in the same region. The disadvantage is that the Great Firewall can make outbound package installs flaky unless you set up a mirror.

issues.original: The dashboard is cleaner, the docs are better, and package mirrors are unnecessary. The tradeoff is latency — if your model provider is Alibaba or Tencent, you add 150ms per turn.
issues.fixed: The dashboard is cleaner, the documentation is better, and package mirrors are unnecessary. The tradeoff is increased latency—150ms per turn—if your model provider is Alibaba or Tencent.

issues.original: The catch is that Hetzner's network is optimized for Europe, so if your users or your model endpoints sit in Asia, you will see the latency.
issues.fixed: The catch is that Hetzner's network is optimized for Europe, so if your users or model endpoints are in Asia, you'll experience higher latency.

issues.original: Free hardware, full control, infinite disk. The problems are uptime (your ISP does not promise five nines), dynamic IPs (you will need DDNS), and port forwarding (which your router may block for inbound 443).
issues.fixed: Free hardware, full control, and unlimited disk space. The downsides include uptime (your ISP doesn't guarantee five nines), dynamic IPs (you'll need DDNS), and port forwarding (which your router may block for inbound 443).

issues.original: A home server works for prototyping; it does not work for production unless you are also your only user.
issues.fixed: A home server is suitable for prototyping but not for production unless you're the only user.

issues.original: Why? The gateway itself uses 200MB of resident memory, but model responses buffer in RAM before streaming to the client, and when a sub-agent forks, the OS duplicates the parent process momentarily. I have watched a 2GB instance OOM during a code-review task that spawned three sub-agents in parallel. 4GB gives you headroom. 8GB eliminates the problem entirely.
issues.fixed: Why? The gateway uses 200MB of resident memory, but model responses buffer in RAM before streaming to the client. When a sub-agent forks, the OS temporarily duplicates the parent process. I've seen a 2GB instance run out of memory during a code review task that spawned three sub-agents in parallel. 4GB provides headroom, while 8GB eliminates the problem entirely.

issues.original: OS: Ubuntu 22.04. The 4G of RAM matters — 2G works for one agent and chokes the moment a sub-agent spawns.
issues.fixed: OS: Ubuntu 22.04. 4GB of RAM is crucial—2GB is sufficient for one agent but becomes insufficient when a sub-agent spawns.

issues.original: pm2 supervises the Gateway so that crashes don't take you down:
issues.fixed: pm2 supervises the Gateway to prevent crashes from taking you down:

issues.original: For the Web Dashboard (port 18789), nginx in front with certs from acme.sh:
issues.fixed: For the Web Dashboard (port 18789), use nginx with certs from acme.sh:

issues.original: The 600s read timeout is not optional — long-running agent turns will exceed the nginx default of 60s and fail mid-stream.
issues.fixed: The 600-second read timeout is necessary—long-running agent turns will exceed the nginx default of 60 seconds and fail mid-stream.

issues.original: If you prefer containers, OpenClaw ships with Docker support. Here is the compose file I use for multi-service deployments:
issues.fixed: If you prefer containers, OpenClaw supports Docker. Here is the compose file I use for multi-service deployments:

issues.original: Three volumes are critical:
issues.fixed: Three volumes are essential:

issues.original: Without this mount, every container restart wipes your setup.
issues.fixed: Without this mount, each container restart erases your setup.

issues.original: Losing this means the agent forgets everything between restarts.
issues.fixed: Losing this means the agent forgets everything between restarts.

issues.original: If you mount this read-only, the agent can read skills but cannot write new ones during self-improvement.
issues.fixed: If you mount this read-only, the agent can read skills but can't write new ones during self-improvement.

issues.original: You run OpenClaw alongside other services (a database, a vector store, a monitoring stack) and you want one [CODE] to bring everything online. The isolation also makes it easier to test config changes — spin up a second container with a different config, compare behavior, kill the worse one.
issues.fixed: You run OpenClaw alongside other services (like a database, vector store, or monitoring stack) and want a single command to bring everything online. The isolation also makes it easier to test config changes—spin up a second container with a different config, compare behavior, and kill the worse one.

issues.original: Debugging file permissions — the container runs as root, your host files may not be, and you will spend time with [CODE]. Inspecting logs requires [CODE] or volume mounts. Hot-reloading skills during development is slower because the filesystem sync has a delay. For a single-service deploy where you ssh into the box and tail logs directly, bare-metal is simpler.
issues.fixed: Debugging file permissions—the container runs as root, but your host files may not, leading to permission issues. Inspecting logs requires [CODE] or volume mounts. Hot-reloading skills during development is slower due to filesystem sync delays. For a single-service deploy where you SSH into the box and tail logs directly, bare-metal is simpler.
